1.3:
25. all ... Israel--

the spiritual Israel ( Ro 2:29 ) and the literal Israel, that is, the final remnant which shall all be saved ( Is 45:17 ; Ro 11:26 ). treated as if they were just, through Christ's righteousness and death ( Jr 23:5 ). literally, "sing" in His praise ( Jr 9:24 ; 1Co 1:31 ).
1.9:
4. old age--

As "your"--"you"--"you," are not in the Hebrew, the sentiment is more general than English Version, though of course it includes the Jews from the infancy to the more advanced age of their history ( Is 47:6 ). that is, the same ( Psa 102:27 ; Joh 8:24 ; He 13:8 ). Not only do I not need to be borne and carried Myself, as the idols ( Is 46:1 ).
1.15:
9. former--

namely, proofs of the sole Godship of Jehovah, from predictions fulfilled, and interpositions of God in behalf of Israel ( Is 45:5 ).

2.6:
5. Sit--

the posture of mourning ( Ezr 9:4 ; Job 2:13 ; La 2:10 ). mourning and misery ( La 3:2 ; Mi 7:8 ). mistress of the world ( Is 13:19 ).
2.12:
10. wickedness--

as in Is 13:11 , the cruelty with which Babylon treated its subject states. ( Psa 10:11 ; 94:7 ). "There is none to exact punishment from me." Sinners are not safe, though seeming secret. astrological and political ( Is 19:11 , &c., as to Egypt). turns thee aside from the right and safe path.
2.18:
15. Thus, &c.--

Such shall be the fate of those astrologers who cost thee such an amount of trouble and money. that is, with whom thou hast trafficked from thy earliest history, the foreigners sojourning in Babylon for the sake of commerce ( Is 13:14 ; Jr 51:6 , 9 ; Na 3:16 , 17 ) [Barnes]. Rather, the astrologers, with whom Babylon had so many dealings ( Is 47:12-14 ) [Horsley]. literally, "straight before him" ( Eze 1:9 , 12 ). The foreigners, whether soothsayers or merchants, shall flee home out of Babylon ( Jr 50:16 ).
2.24:
4. obstinate--

Hebrew, "hard" ( De 9:27 ; Eze 3:7 , Margin). inflexible ( Ac 7:51 ). shameless as a harlot (see Jr 6:28 ; 3:3 ; Eze 3:7 , Margin).
2.30:
9. refrain--

literally, "muzzle"; His wrath, after the return, was to be restrained a while, and then, because of their sins, let loose again ( Psa 78:38 ). that is, mine anger towards thee.

3.3:
the first ... last--

12-15. The Almighty, who has founded heaven and earth, can, and will, restore His people. ( Is 41:4 ; 44:6 ).
3.9:
19. sand--

retaining the metaphor of "the sea" ( Is 48:18 ). rather, as the Hebrew, "like that (the offspring) of its (the sea's) bowels"; referring to the countless living creatures, fishes, &c., of the sea, rather than the gravel [Maurer]. Jerome, Chaldee, and Syriac support English Version. transition from the second person, "thy," to the third "his." Israel's name was cut off "as a nation" during the Babylonish captivity; also it is so now, to which the prophecy especially looks ( Ro 11:20 ).
3.15:
1. O isles--

Messiah, as the ideal Israel ( Is 49:3 ), states the object of His mission, His want of success for a time, yet His certainty of ultimate success. Messiah is here regarded as having been rejected by the Jews ( Is 49:4 , 5 ), and as now turning to the Gentiles, to whom the Father hath given Him "for a light and salvation." "Isles" mean all regions beyond sea. ( Is 44:2 ; Lu 1:31 ; Joh 10:36 ). His name "Jesus" (that is, God-Saviour) was designated by God before His birth ( Mt 1:21 ).
3.21:
6. It is a light thing--

"It is too little that Thou shouldest," [Hengstenberg], that is, It is not enough honor to Thee to raise up Jacob and Israel, but I design for Thee more, namely, that Thou shouldest be the means of enlightening the Gentiles ( Is 42:6 , 7 ; 60:3 ). namely, those remaining after the judgments of God on the nation--the elect remnant of Israel reserved for mercy. Lowth, with a slight but needless change of the Hebrew, translates for "tribes" and "preserved," the "scions"--the "branches."
